meta.name: Filesystem Implementation
meta.description: Inode-based filesystem with block allocation, directories, FUSE integration, and write-ahead journaling
meta.essence: Block-level storage abstraction layered with inode-based metadata structures for file organization, directory tree traversal through linked entries, and crash consistency achieved through write-ahead journaling that serializes filesystem operations to a sequential log before applying changes to primary data structures.
meta.why_important: Building a filesystem teaches you how operating systems manage persistent storage at the lowest level — skills directly applicable to database internals, storage systems engineering, and understanding performance bottlenecks in any I/O-intensive application.
learning_outcomes: Design and implement on-disk layout with superblock, bitmaps, inode table, and data blocks
Implement inode-based file metadata with direct and indirect block pointers
Build directory tree operations with path resolution and entry management
Implement file read, write, truncate, and sparse file handling
Integrate with FUSE to mount the filesystem as a real mount point
Implement write-ahead journaling for crash consistency and recovery
Build mkfs formatter initializing filesystem structures on a raw image

milestones.name: Block Layer and mkfs
milestones.description: Implement raw block device abstraction, on-disk layout design, free block bitmap, and mkfs formatter to initialize a fresh filesystem.
acceptance_criteria: Block device abstraction provides read_block(block_num) and write_block(block_num, data) over a fixed 4KB block size backed by a disk image file
On-disk layout is defined: superblock (block 0), block bitmap, inode bitmap, inode table, journal region, data blocks — all positions derived from superblock metadata
Superblock stores magic number, filesystem version, total blocks, total inodes, block size, free block/inode counts, and journal region location
Block bitmap tracks free/used status for all data blocks; allocation returns the first free block and marks it used; deallocation clears the bit
Inode bitmap tracks free/used status for all inodes independently from blocks
mkfs tool initializes a raw file: writes superblock, zeroes bitmaps, initializes inode table, creates root directory (inode 0 or 1) with . and .. entries
After mkfs, the filesystem image can be read back and all metadata is consistent
pitfalls: Not initializing the superblock magic number; mounting without validation risks interpreting garbage as filesystem metadata
Bitmap corruption from not flushing writes; a crash between allocation and data write leaves orphaned blocks
Off-by-one in bitmap indexing; block 0 is the superblock, data blocks start at a different offset
Writing beyond the disk image size without validation; check total_blocks boundary
deliverables: Block device abstraction with read/write operations on a file-backed image
Superblock structure and serialization
Block bitmap allocator and deallocator
Inode bitmap allocator and deallocator
mkfs tool creating a formatted filesystem image with root directory

milestones.name: Inode Management
milestones.description: Implement inode structure for file metadata with direct and indirect block pointers for variable-size files.
acceptance_criteria: Inode structure contains: file type (regular, directory), size, permissions (mode), link count, timestamps (atime, mtime, ctime), direct block pointers (e.g., 12), single-indirect pointer, and double-indirect pointer
Direct block pointers address the first N blocks of a file (e.g., first 12 × 4KB = 48KB)
Single-indirect pointer references a block containing an array of block pointers, extending file capacity (e.g., +4MB)
Double-indirect pointer references a block of single-indirect pointers, further extending capacity (e.g., +4GB)
Inode allocation from the inode bitmap returns a free inode number; deallocation frees all associated data blocks AND indirect blocks
Inode read/write serializes inode structure to/from the inode table region on disk
Null/zero block pointers in the inode indicate unallocated blocks (holes for sparse files)
Timestamps are updated on inode modification (mtime, ctime) and access (atime)
pitfalls: Leaking indirect blocks when freeing an inode; must traverse and free single-indirect and double-indirect blocks recursively
Off-by-one in block offset calculations when transitioning from direct to indirect pointers
Not distinguishing between zero block pointer (hole/unallocated) and block 0 (superblock); block 0 should never be a data block
Forgetting to update link count and timestamps on inode operations
concepts: Sparse file representation with null pointers
deliverables: Inode structure with all metadata fields
Direct block pointer access for small files
Single-indirect and double-indirect pointer traversal for large files
Inode allocation and full deallocation (including indirect blocks)
Inode serialization to/from disk

milestones.name: Directory Operations
milestones.description: Implement directory entries, path resolution, and directory management.
acceptance_criteria: Directory is a special file whose data blocks contain an array of directory entries; each entry has inode number, entry length, name length, and name
add_entry(dir_inode, name, target_inode) appends a new entry to the directory; fails if name already exists
remove_entry(dir_inode, name) removes the entry and decrements the target inode's link count; if link count reaches 0 and no open file descriptors, inode is freed
Path resolution traverses '/' separated components from root (or cwd): "for path 'a/b/c', look up 'a' in root, then 'b' in a's inode, then 'c' in b's inode"
'.' entry always points to the directory's own inode; '..' entry points to parent directory's inode (root's '..' points to itself)
mkdir creates a new directory inode with '.' and '..' entries; parent's link count is incremented (because '..' in child points to parent)
rmdir removes a directory only if it's empty (contains only '.' and '..')
Hard link creation (link) adds a new directory entry pointing to an existing inode and increments link count
pitfalls: Not validating name uniqueness before adding an entry; duplicate names cause ambiguous lookups
Forgetting to increment parent link count on mkdir (for child's '..' entry) and decrement on rmdir
Not handling the root directory's '..' correctly (it must point to itself)
Race conditions in concurrent directory modifications; for FUSE, multiple threads may modify simultaneously
Maximum filename length must be enforced; long names can overflow entry structures
deliverables: Directory entry structure and serialization
add_entry and remove_entry operations
Path-to-inode resolution function
mkdir and rmdir with link count management
Hard link (link) operation

milestones.name: File Read/Write Operations
milestones.description: Implement file creation, reading, writing, truncation, and sparse file support.
acceptance_criteria: create_file(parent_dir_inode, name, mode) allocates a new inode, adds a directory entry, and returns the inode number
read(inode, offset, length) reads data from the file's blocks via inode pointers; reading unallocated blocks (holes) returns zero bytes
write(inode, offset, data) writes data to allocated blocks; allocates new blocks as needed for growth; updates inode size if writing past current end
truncate(inode, new_size) either extends the file (zero-filling conceptually via holes) or shrinks it (freeing blocks beyond new_size and updating pointers)
Sparse file support: writing at a high offset without writing intermediate positions does not allocate blocks for the gap; those blocks read as zeros
Append operation writes data at the current end of file and extends inode size
File metadata (size, mtime, ctime) is updated correctly on every write and truncate operation
Unit tests verify round-trip: write data, read it back, compare byte-for-byte
pitfalls: Not zero-filling partial blocks when writing doesn't align to block boundaries
Forgetting to free indirect pointer blocks when truncating below their range
Memory leaks from caching block data without eviction
Off-by-one in calculating which block index corresponds to a given file offset
deliverables: create_file operation allocating inode and adding directory entry
read operation mapping file offset to blocks and returning data
write operation allocating blocks and writing data
truncate operation shrinking or extending files
Sparse file handling with zero-filled reads for holes
Unit test suite for file operations

milestones.name: FUSE Integration
milestones.description: Mount the filesystem via FUSE for real-world testing with standard Unix tools (ls, cat, cp, etc.).
acceptance_criteria: Filesystem mounts via FUSE appearing as a regular mount point accessible by the OS
FUSE callbacks implemented: getattr, readdir, lookup, create, open, read, write, release, mkdir, rmdir, unlink, rename, truncate, chmod, utimens
Standard tools work: "'ls -la' lists directory contents with correct sizes/permissions; 'cat' reads files; 'cp' copies files into the mount; 'echo > file' writes"
Concurrent access from multiple processes does not corrupt filesystem state (proper locking around shared structures)
Unmount flushes all pending data to the disk image and cleans up resources
rename operation moves entries between directories atomically
pitfalls: Not implementing getattr correctly causes ls and every other tool to fail (it's called on every path operation)
Deadlocks in FUSE callbacks when holding locks and calling back into the filesystem
FUSE passes paths, not inodes, to most callbacks; each callback must resolve the path, which is expensive without caching
Forgetting to implement 'create' vs 'mknod'; FUSE calls create for O_CREAT opens
Not flushing dirty data on unmount loses recently written data
deliverables: FUSE operation callback registration
All required callbacks (getattr, readdir, create, read, write, mkdir, rmdir, unlink, rename, truncate, etc.)
Locking strategy for thread-safe access
Mount/unmount lifecycle with flush-on-unmount
Integration test running standard Unix commands against mounted filesystem

milestones.name: Write-Ahead Journaling and Crash Recovery
milestones.description: Implement a write-ahead journal for crash consistency. Metadata operations are journaled so the filesystem can recover from crashes without corruption.
acceptance_criteria: Journal region occupies a fixed set of blocks on disk (configured in superblock); journal entries are written sequentially with wrap-around
Each filesystem operation (create, write metadata, mkdir, unlink, etc.) is wrapped in a transaction: begin → journal writes → commit → apply to data structures → end
Transaction commit writes a commit record to the journal; without a commit record, the transaction is considered incomplete on recovery
Recovery (fsck/replay): on mount, scan the journal for committed transactions and replay their operations; discard uncommitted transactions
Metadata journaling mode: only metadata changes (inode updates, bitmap changes, directory entries) are journaled; data blocks are written directly
Crash simulation test: kill the filesystem process mid-operation, remount, verify recovery produces a consistent filesystem with no orphaned inodes or leaked blocks
Journal checkpoint: "after all committed transactions are applied, the journal is marked as clean so it doesn't replay stale entries"
pitfalls: Not writing the commit record atomically (or at least ensuring it's fully flushed); a partial commit record looks uncommitted on recovery
Replaying a transaction that was already applied (idempotency): operations must be safe to apply multiple times
Journal wrap-around overwriting uncommitted transactions; must checkpoint before the journal fills
Data journaling (journaling data blocks too) doubles write amplification; metadata-only journaling is the practical default
Not fsyncing the journal before writing to the main data structures; reorder by the OS can defeat the write-ahead guarantee
deliverables: Journal region with sequential log entries and wrap-around
Transaction API (begin, journal_write, commit, end)
Journal replay on mount for crash recovery
Checkpoint operation marking journal as clean after replay
Crash simulation test verifying filesystem consistency after interrupted operations

blueprint.title: Filesystem Implementation
blueprint.overview: Build a complete inode-based filesystem from the ground up, starting with raw block I/O and culminating in a FUSE-mountable filesystem with crash-consistent journaling. This project strips away the abstraction layers between your code and persistent storage, revealing how operating systems transform a bag of bytes on disk into the hierarchical file trees we navigate daily. You'll implement every layer: block allocation with bitmaps, inode metadata with multi-level indirect pointers, directory entry management, file I/O with sparse file support, and write-ahead journaling that survives crashes. By the end, you'll understand why databases, container runtimes, and storage engines all share the same fundamental concerns about block alignment, atomic writes, and recovery semantics.
blueprint.design_philosophy: Filesystems exist at the intersection of hardware constraints (block sizes, seek latency, write ordering) and user expectations (arbitrary file sizes, hierarchical organization, crash safety). This project teaches by forcing you to negotiate these tensions directly. Every design decision—where to place the inode table, how many direct pointers to use, when to checkpoint the journal—has measurable consequences for performance, reliability, and correctness. The milestone progression mirrors the dependency graph: you cannot implement directories without inodes, you cannot implement journaling without understanding what metadata changes must be atomic, and you cannot test with real tools without FUSE integration. Each layer reveals why the next layer's constraints exist.

implementation.rationale: Filesystems demand direct control over memory layout, byte-level serialization, and pointer arithmetic for multi-level indirect block traversal. C's struct layout is deterministic, enabling precise on-disk format definitions that match byte-for-byte across compiles. Manual memory management is essential for implementing block caches and buffer pools without GC pauses. The FUSE library has first-class C bindings, and the debugging process requires familiarity with tools like gdb, valgrind, and hexdump—skills reinforced by C development.
implementation.style_guide: snake_case for functions and variables; UPPER_CASE for constants and macros; struct names prefixed with fs_ (e.g., fs_superblock, fs_inode). All on-disk structures must be packed (#pragma pack) with explicit padding fields for alignment. Every function that can fail returns an int error code (0 for success, negative for errors). Block numbers use type uint32_t or uint64_t consistently. All file I/O uses the block device abstraction—no direct fread/fwrite on the image file after initialization.
implementation.build_system: Makefile with separate targets for mkfs, fs-fuse, and test binaries. Debug builds with -g -O0 -fsanitize=address,undefined; release builds with -O2. The image file is generated by 'make format' which runs mkfs.
alternatives: Rust (memory safety benefits, but FUSE bindings are less mature; more abstraction overhead for low-level bit manipulation)
Go (simpler FUSE integration via hanwen/go-fuse, but GC makes cache behavior unpredictable and struct serialization requires explicit handling)

assumed_known: C programming with pointers, structs, and memory allocation
File I/O basics (open, read, write, seek, close)
Basic data structures (arrays, linked lists, hash tables conceptually)
Unix file permissions and directory structure
Command-line comfort (ls, cat, mkdir, rm, mount)

must_teach_first.explanation: Storage hardware reads/writes in fixed-size blocks (traditionally 512 bytes, now often 4KB). Filesystems must align data to these boundaries for correct operation.
On-disk structures must use consistent byte ordering (usually little-endian on x86) for portability across systems.
Files can have unallocated regions (holes) that read as zeros without consuming disk space, enabling efficient representation of large sparse data.
A write operation must either complete fully or not at all; partial writes from crashes leave systems in undefined states.

milestones.title: Block Layer and mkfs
milestones.summary: Implement the raw block device abstraction, define the on-disk layout (superblock, bitmaps, inode table, journal, data blocks), build bitmap-based allocation, and create the mkfs formatter that initializes a fresh filesystem image.
milestones.misconception: Developers often think filesystems are just 'files stored in folders' and that formatting a disk is trivial—you just write some header. In reality, a filesystem is a carefully choreographed dance of metadata structures at specific byte offsets. The superblock must be at a known location (block 0). Bitmaps must cover exactly the right number of blocks with no gaps. The inode table size must be calculated from the expected file count. A single off-by-one in any of these calculations corrupts everything that follows.
milestones.reveal: The 'format' operation (mkfs) is actually creating a complete skeleton of the filesystem: partitioning the raw bytes into regions, initializing the root directory, and establishing the invariant that EVERY subsequent operation must maintain. The filesystem isn't 'created' when you write files—it's created once by mkfs, and every file operation is a careful mutation of that initial state. The block bitmap is the source of truth for allocation; the inode bitmap is the source of truth for metadata. Neither can be reconstructed if corrupted.
cascade: Database storage engines — they also use block-based layouts, bitmaps for page allocation, and superblock-like header pages. The skills transfer directly to implementing a buffer pool or page allocator.
RAID and volume managers — understanding how block devices can be layered (LVM, mdadm) requires grasping this same abstraction: a linear address space of blocks with metadata at fixed locations.

Flash storage and wear leveling — SSDs internally maintain their own block mapping tables, conceptually similar to filesystem bitmaps, but with additional complexity for garbage collection and wear distribution.
Memory allocators (jemalloc/tcmalloc) — they use bitmap-based allocation for pages and slabs, with free lists and boundary tags serving the same role as our block bitmap.
Disk partitioning (MBR/GPT) — the partition table is itself a filesystem-like structure at a fixed offset (LBA 0 for MBR, LBA 1 for GPT header), with its own checksums and backup copies.
yaml_acceptance_criteria: Block device abstraction provides read_block(block_num) and write_block(block_num, data) over a fixed 4KB block size backed by a disk image file
On-disk layout is defined: superblock (block 0), block bitmap, inode bitmap, inode table, journal region, data blocks — all positions derived from superblock metadata
Superblock stores magic number, filesystem version, total blocks, total inodes, block size, free block/inode counts, and journal region location
Block bitmap tracks free/used status for all data blocks; allocation returns the first free block and marks it used; deallocation clears the bit
Inode bitmap tracks free/used status for all inodes independently from blocks
mkfs tool initializes a raw file: writes superblock, zeroes bitmaps, initializes inode table, creates root directory (inode 0 or 1) with . and .. entries
After mkfs, the filesystem image can be read back and all metadata is consistent

diagrams.title: Filesystem Component Atlas
diagrams.description: Satellite view showing all six milestones as interconnected regions: Block Layer at the foundation, supporting Inode Management and Directory Operations, which enable File Read/Write, all exposed through FUSE Integration, with Journaling wrapping metadata operations for crash safety.

diagrams.title: On-Disk Layout: Block Map
diagrams.description: Street-level view of the filesystem image showing block 0 (superblock), block 1-N (block bitmap), block N+1 to M (inode bitmap), inode table region, journal region, and data block region. Each region is labeled with its purpose and size calculation.

diagrams.title: Superblock Structure Layout
diagrams.description: Microscopic view of the superblock struct showing byte offsets for each field: magic (4B), version (4B), block_size (4B), total_blocks (8B), total_inodes (8B), free_blocks (8B), free_inodes (8B), bitmap_start (8B), inode_table_start (8B), journal_start (8B), journal_size (8B), data_start (8B), checksum (4B). Total: 84B within 4KB block.

diagrams.title: Bitmap Allocation Walkthrough
diagrams.description: Step-by-step trace showing: (1) initial bitmap state with bits 0-2 used, (2) scan for first zero bit (bit 3), (3) set bit 3 to 1, (4) return block number. Contrast with deallocation: receive block number, calculate bit index, clear bit.

diagrams.title: mkfs Initialization Sequence
diagrams.description: Before/after view of a raw file before mkfs (all zeros or garbage) and after mkfs (superblock at 0, bitmaps initialized with root inode marked used, inode table with root directory inode initialized, journal region zeroed, root directory data block with . and .. entries).

diagrams.title: Block Number to Byte Offset
diagrams.description: Data flow diagram showing: block_num → multiply by BLOCK_SIZE (4096) → byte_offset. Example: block 42 → byte offset 172032. Critical for read_block and write_block implementation.

diagrams.title: Root Directory Initialization Detail
diagrams.description: Structure layout showing inode 1 (root) with type=directory, size=4096 (one block), block[0]=first_data_block. The data block contains two directory entries: '.' (inode 1) and '..' (inode 1, self-reference for root).

milestones.title: Inode Management
milestones.summary: Design and implement the inode structure containing file metadata and block pointers. Implement direct block access for small files, single-indirect and double-indirect pointer traversal for large files, and proper allocation/deallocation of all associated blocks.
milestones.misconception: Many developers think files are 'stored in inodes' or that an inode contains the file's actual data. In reality, an inode is pure metadata—a fixed-size record containing pointers to data blocks. The data lives elsewhere. Another misconception is that file size is limited by available disk space; actually, it's limited by the addressing capability of the pointer structure (12 direct + 1 single-indirect + 1 double-indirect = specific max file size).
milestones.reveal: The multi-level indirect pointer scheme is a brilliant compression trick. With 12 direct pointers (48KB), a single-indirect block (1024 more pointers = 4MB), and a double-indirect block (1024 × 1024 = 4GB), a file can grow to ~4GB using only 14 pointers in the inode itself. The indirect blocks are allocated lazily—only when the file grows past the direct pointer range. This means small files (under 48KB) require only the inode, while large files trade pointer indirection for storage efficiency. Null pointers represent holes (sparse regions), not errors.
cascade: Database B-trees — the indirect block traversal is essentially a 2-level B-tree where inode pointers are the root, indirect blocks are internal nodes, and data blocks are leaves. Database page management uses the same fan-out calculations.

Virtual memory page tables — the page directory → page table → page frame hierarchy is isomorphic to double-indirect → single-indirect → data block. Both translate logical addresses through fixed-size pointer arrays.

Filesystem fragmentation — as files grow and shrink, the blocks pointed to by consecutive pointers may become non-contiguous. Understanding indirect blocks explains why defragmentation tools exist and why sequential reads can become random I/O.
Cloud storage object addressing — object stores like S3 conceptually use a similar indirection: key → metadata → data location. The 'inode' is the metadata, 'blocks' are the object chunks.
Sparse matrix storage — the hole/allocated-block distinction in files is exactly how sparse matrices store only non-zero elements with index metadata.
yaml_acceptance_criteria: Inode structure contains: file type (regular, directory), size, permissions (mode), link count, timestamps (atime, mtime, ctime), direct block pointers (e.g., 12), single-indirect pointer, and double-indirect pointer
Direct block pointers address the first N blocks of a file (e.g., first 12 × 4KB = 48KB)
Single-indirect pointer references a block containing an array of block pointers, extending file capacity (e.g., +4MB)
Double-indirect pointer references a block of single-indirect pointers, further extending capacity (e.g., +4GB)
Inode allocation from the inode bitmap returns a free inode number; deallocation frees all associated data blocks AND indirect blocks
Inode read/write serializes inode structure to/from the inode table region on disk
Null/zero block pointers in the inode indicate unallocated blocks (holes for sparse files)
Timestamps are updated on inode modification (mtime, ctime) and access (atime)

diagrams.title: Inode Structure Memory Layout
diagrams.description: Microscopic view showing byte offsets: mode (2B), type (2B), link_count (2B), padding (2B), size (8B), atime (8B), mtime (8B), ctime (8B), direct[0-11] (48B), single_indirect (4B), double_indirect (4B), reserved (20B). Total: 128B per inode.

diagrams.title: Direct, Single, Double Indirect Pointers
diagrams.description: Tree diagram showing inode at root with 12 direct pointers to data blocks (labeled 'direct 0-11'), 1 pointer to single-indirect block containing 1024 data block pointers, and 1 pointer to double-indirect block containing 1024 single-indirect block pointers, each containing 1024 data block pointers.

diagrams.title: File Size to Pointer Type Mapping
diagrams.description: Number line showing: 0-48KB (direct pointers only), 48KB-4MB (direct + single-indirect), 4MB-4GB (all three levels). Each range is annotated with the number of block accesses needed to reach data: 1 for direct, 2 for single-indirect, 3 for double-indirect.

diagrams.title: File Offset to Block Resolution
diagrams.description: Trace example showing: offset 5MB → exceeds direct (48KB) and single-indirect (4MB) → use double-indirect. Calculate: subtract 4MB, divide by 4KB → need block index 256 in double-indirect range. Double-indirect[0] → single-indirect[256/1024=0] → data_block[256%1024=256].

diagrams.title: Inode Deallocation: Recursive Block Free
diagrams.description: State evolution diagram showing: inode with 3 direct blocks, 1 single-indirect (with 2 data blocks), 1 double-indirect (with 1 single-indirect, with 1 data block). Step-by-step: free direct blocks, free data blocks from single-indirect, free single-indirect block, traverse double-indirect, free nested blocks, free double-indirect blocks, clear inode, mark inode free in bitmap.

diagrams.title: Inode Table in On-Disk Layout
diagrams.description: Street view showing inode table region between inode bitmap and journal. Inode table starts at block X, contains N inodes (calculated from filesystem size). Each inode is 128B, so ~32 inodes per 4KB block. Example: 10000 inodes = 313 blocks for inode table.

diagrams.title: Timestamp Update Rules
diagrams.description: Decision matrix showing which operations update which timestamps: read() → atime; write() → mtime, ctime; chmod/chown → ctime only; create → all three; link/unlink → ctime of parent, mtime/ctime of affected inode.

milestones.title: Directory Operations
milestones.summary: Implement directory entries as special files containing name-to-inode mappings. Build path resolution that traverses the directory tree, implement add/remove entry operations, manage link counts for hard links, and handle mkdir/rmdir with proper parent-child relationships.
milestones.misconception: Developers often think directories 'contain' files, like a folder in a file cabinet. In reality, a directory IS a file—a special file whose data blocks contain an array of directory entries. Each entry is just a tuple of (name, inode_number). The 'hierarchy' is an emergent property of these name→inode mappings. The inode numbers are what's real; the paths are just convenient names for humans.
milestones.reveal: Path resolution is a recursive lookup: to find '/a/b/c', you read the root directory's data blocks, scan for entry 'a', get inode N, read N's data blocks (which are a directory), scan for 'b', get inode M, read M's data blocks, scan for 'c', get inode P. Each step is a disk read (or cache lookup). The '.' and '..' entries are not special magic—they're just directory entries that happen to point to the current directory and parent directory. For root, '..' points to itself. Hard links are the same mechanism: multiple directory entries pointing to the same inode, with a link count tracking how many references exist.
cascade: Symbolic links vs hard links — symlinks store a path string as file data, resolved at access time. Hard links share the same inode. This explains why symlinks can dangle (target deleted) but hard links keep data alive until all links are removed.
DNS resolution — the hierarchical path resolution (root → top-level domain → subdomain) is isomorphic to directory traversal. DNS caches correspond to directory entry caches (dentry cache in Linux).

Package managers and dependency graphs — directories as name→inode maps are similar to package name→version maps. The 'dependency hell' of conflicting requirements mirrors directory structure constraints.

Garbage collection reference counting — the link count is exactly a reference count. When it reaches zero, the inode (and its blocks) can be freed. This is how Python's GC works for objects with multiple references.
Database indexes — a directory is essentially a dense index mapping names to inode numbers. Database B-tree indexes serve the same lookup function for table rows.
yaml_acceptance_criteria: Directory is a special file whose data blocks contain an array of directory entries; each entry has inode number, entry length, name length, and name
add_entry(dir_inode, name, target_inode) appends a new entry to the directory; fails if name already exists
remove_entry(dir_inode, name) removes the entry and decrements the target inode's link count; if link count reaches 0 and no open file descriptors, inode is freed
Path resolution traverses '/' separated components from root (or cwd): "for path 'a/b/c', look up 'a' in root, then 'b' in a's inode, then 'c' in b's inode"
'.' entry always points to the directory's own inode; '..' entry points to parent directory's inode (root's '..' points to itself)
mkdir creates a new directory inode with '.' and '..' entries; parent's link count is incremented (because '..' in child points to parent)
rmdir removes a directory only if it's empty (contains only '.' and '..')
Hard link creation (link) adds a new directory entry pointing to an existing inode and increments link count

diagrams.title: Directory Entry Structure
diagrams.description: Microscopic view of a directory entry showing byte offsets: inode (4B), entry_len (2B), name_len (2B), name (variable, up to 255B). Entry_len includes padding to 4-byte boundary. Example entry for 'hello.txt': inode=42, entry_len=16, name_len=9, name='hello.txt\0' + 2B padding.

diagrams.title: Directory Data Block Contents
diagrams.description: Street view showing a directory's data block containing multiple directory entries laid out linearly: '.' (inode 5), '..' (inode 2), 'foo' (inode 12), 'bar.txt' (inode 15), 'subdir' (inode 20). Each entry has variable length based on filename.

diagrams.title: Path Resolution Walkthrough
diagrams.description: Trace example for '/home/user/docs/file.txt': (1) read root directory inode (always inode 1 or 2), (2) scan root data blocks for 'home', get inode 100, (3) read inode 100's data blocks, scan for 'user', get inode 200, (4) read inode 200's data blocks, scan for 'docs', get inode 300, (5) read inode 300's data blocks, scan for 'file.txt', get inode 400. Return inode 400.

diagrams.title: Hard Links: Multiple Names, One Inode
diagrams.description: Before/after showing: directory /a contains 'file1' → inode 50. After link('/a/file1', '/b/file2'): directory /a has 'file1' → inode 50, directory /b has 'file2' → inode 50. Inode 50's link_count changes from 1 to 2. Data blocks are shared.

diagrams.title: mkdir State Changes
diagrams.description: State evolution showing: (1) allocate new inode 75, (2) allocate data block 1000, (3) initialize block 1000 with '.' → inode 75 and '..' → parent_inode, (4) add entry 'newdir' → inode 75 to parent's data block, (5) increment parent's link_count (for child's '..'), (6) set inode 75's link_count = 2 (for '.' and parent's entry).

diagrams.title: rmdir Validation and Cleanup
diagrams.description: Decision flow: (1) verify target is a directory, (2) read directory entries, (3) if entries > 2 (only '.' and '..'), fail with ENOTEMPTY, (4) remove entry from parent, (5) decrement parent's link_count, (6) free directory's data block, (7) free inode 75.

diagrams.title: Link Count Lifecycle
diagrams.description: State evolution showing link count changes: create file → link_count=1, hard link → link_count=2, another hard link → link_count=3, unlink original → link_count=2, unlink second → link_count=1, unlink last → link_count=0 → inode freed. Contrast with open file descriptor keeping inode alive (delete on last close).

milestones.title: File Read/Write Operations
milestones.summary: Implement file creation, reading, writing, and truncation. Map file offsets to block numbers via inode pointers, allocate blocks on demand during writes, handle sparse files with hole detection, and update metadata correctly.
milestones.misconception: Developers often think file I/O is simple: read N bytes from offset X. The complexity hidden includes: calculating which block(s) contain the requested range, handling reads that span block boundaries, allocating new blocks for writes past file end, zero-filling partial blocks, and representing sparse files efficiently. The 'file size' stored in the inode is metadata, not a physical constraint—you can write at any offset.
milestones.reveal: Reading from a hole (null pointer in inode) doesn't return an error—it returns zeros, synthesized by the filesystem, without any disk I/O. This is how sparse files work: a 1TB file with only 1MB of actual data might use only a few hundred blocks. Writing at offset 1GB in a 1KB file doesn't allocate 1GB of blocks—only the blocks actually written to are allocated. The gap is a hole. Truncation is the inverse: extending creates a conceptual hole (no blocks allocated), shrinking frees blocks beyond the new size and must handle partial-block zeroing.
cascade: Database extent allocation — databases allocate space in extents (contiguous block groups) rather than individual blocks, but the same offset-to-location mapping applies. Sparse files in databases enable efficient NULL column storage.

Memory-mapped files (mmap) — the same offset-to-block mapping is used for page faults. A read from a mapped region triggers the filesystem to load the corresponding block. Holes in files become zero-filled pages.
Log-structured merge trees (LSST) — the compaction process is similar to file truncation: removing old data and freeing blocks. SSTable files are often sparse with tombstones.
Copy-on-write snapshots — when a block is written, a new block is allocated and the pointer updated, preserving the old block for snapshots. This is how ZFS and Btrfs implement atomic snapshots.
Video streaming and range requests — HTTP byte-range requests map directly to file offset-to-block calculations. Sparse video files (with seek tables as holes) optimize streaming.
yaml_acceptance_criteria: create_file(parent_dir_inode, name, mode) allocates a new inode, adds a directory entry, and returns the inode number
read(inode, offset, length) reads data from the file's blocks via inode pointers; reading unallocated blocks (holes) returns zero bytes
write(inode, offset, data) writes data to allocated blocks; allocates new blocks as needed for growth; updates inode size if writing past current end
truncate(inode, new_size) either extends the file (zero-filling conceptually via holes) or shrinks it (freeing blocks beyond new_size and updating pointers)
Sparse file support: writing at a high offset without writing intermediate positions does not allocate blocks for the gap; those blocks read as zeros
Append operation writes data at the current end of file and extends inode size
File metadata (size, mtime, ctime) is updated correctly on every write and truncate operation
Unit tests verify round-trip: write data, read it back, compare byte-for-byte

diagrams.title: File Offset to Block Mapping
diagrams.description: Data flow showing: offset → block_index = offset / BLOCK_SIZE, offset_in_block = offset % BLOCK_SIZE. For read spanning two blocks: read block[block_index], read block[block_index+1], concatenate with offset adjustment.

diagrams.title: Reading Through a Hole
diagrams.description: Trace example: file has blocks 0, 1, 5 allocated (blocks 2-4 are holes). Read offset 8KB, length 12KB. (1) read block 1 (offset 8-12KB), (2) detect block 2 is null → return zeros (12-16KB), (3) detect block 3 is null → return zeros (16-20KB), (4) read block 5 (20KB+).

diagrams.title: Write Block Allocation on Demand
diagrams.description: State evolution: write at offset 16KB to empty file. (1) calculate block 4 needed, (2) check inode.direct[4] is null, (3) allocate block 500 from bitmap, (4) set inode.direct[4] = 500, (5) write data to block 500, (6) update inode.size = 20KB.

diagrams.title: Write Spanning Multiple Blocks
diagrams.description: Trace showing write of 10KB at offset 2KB in a file: (1) write 2KB to block 0 (partial, offset 2-4KB), (2) write 4KB to block 1 (full), (3) write 4KB to block 2 (full), (4) write 0KB to block 3 (partial, but data exhausted). Note: block 3 not needed for this write.

diagrams.title: Truncate Down: Block Freeing
diagrams.description: Before/after showing: file with blocks 0-10 allocated, size=44KB. Truncate to 16KB: (1) set inode.size=16KB, (2) free blocks 4-10, (3) clear direct[4-10] to null, (4) if indirect blocks become empty, free them too.

diagrams.title: Truncate Up: Creating Holes
diagrams.description: Before/after: file with size=4KB, truncate to 1MB. No new blocks allocated. inode.size=1MB. Reads between 4KB and 1MB return zeros (hole). Only if written to will blocks be allocated.

diagrams.Title: Partial Block Zero-Filling
diagrams.description: Microscopic view: writing 1KB to fresh block. (1) allocate block, (2) zero entire 4KB block first, (3) copy 1KB to offset 0, (4) remaining 3KB stays zero. Critical for security (no data leakage from previously freed blocks).

diagrams.title: Read-Write Roundtrip Test Flow
diagrams.description: Test sequence: (1) create file, (2) write 'Hello World' at offset 0, (3) write 'Goodbye' at offset 20, (4) read offset 0, length 27, (5) verify bytes 0-10='Hello World', bytes 11-19=\0, bytes 20-26='Goodbye'.

milestones.title: FUSE Integration
milestones.summary: Mount the filesystem via FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) to make it accessible as a real mount point. Implement all required FUSE callbacks, handle concurrent access with proper locking, and validate with standard Unix tools.
milestones.misconception: Developers think filesystems must be in the kernel to work. FUSE proves otherwise: the kernel VFS layer forwards operations to a userspace daemon via /dev/fuse. The daemon handles each request (get a directory listing, read a file) and responds. The kernel just marshals data. This means your Python script can be a filesystem—but it also means every file operation involves a kernel↔userspace context switch.
milestones.reveal: FUSE callbacks receive paths, not inodes. When you do 'cat /mnt/myfs/a/b/c.txt', FUSE calls your getattr('/a/b/c.txt'), then open('/a/b/c.txt'), then read('/a/b/c.txt', offset, size). Each callback must resolve the path from scratch (unless you cache). This is expensive but flexible. The getattr callback is called constantly—every ls, every tab-completion, every stat. It must be fast and correct. Locking is critical: multiple threads handle concurrent requests, and your internal state must remain consistent.
cascade: Network filesystems (NFS, SMB, 9P) — they use similar callback architectures, just over the network. The VFS layer abstracts whether the filesystem is local kernel, local FUSE, or remote.
Container filesystems (overlayfs, UnionFS) — these layer multiple filesystems with copy-on-up semantics. FUSE can implement similar layering in userspace.
Cloud storage gateways (s3fs) — mount S3 buckets as local filesystems using FUSE. The callbacks translate file operations to S3 API calls.
Version control filesystems (GitFS) — mount a git repository as a filesystem where branches are directories. FUSE enables creative reinterpretations of 'file'.
Security sandboxes (sandboxfs) — use FUSE to present a filtered/composed view of the filesystem to sandboxed processes, hiding sensitive paths.
yaml_acceptance_criteria: Filesystem mounts via FUSE appearing as a regular mount point accessible by the OS
FUSE callbacks implemented: getattr, readdir, lookup, create, open, read, write, release, mkdir, rmdir, unlink, rename, truncate, chmod, utimens
Standard tools work: 'ls -la' lists directory contents with correct sizes/permissions; 'cat' reads files; 'cp' copies files into the mount; 'echo > file' writes
Concurrent access from multiple processes does not corrupt filesystem state (proper locking around shared structures)
Unmount flushes all pending data to the disk image and cleans up resources
rename operation moves entries between directories atomically

diagrams.title: FUSE Request Flow Architecture
diagrams.description: Satellite view showing: User process (ls) → VFS (kernel) → FUSE kernel module → /dev/fuse → FUSE library (libfuse) → User's callback functions → Our filesystem code → Block device → Disk image. Response flows back through same path.

diagrams.title: FUSE Callback Lifecycle for 'cat file'
diagrams.description: Trace sequence: (1) getattr('/file') → return stat struct, (2) open('/file') → return file handle, (3) read('/file', offset=0, size=4096) → return data, (4) read('/file', offset=4096, ...) → return more data, (5) release('/file') → cleanup.

diagrams.title: getattr: The Most Critical Callback
diagrams.description: Data flow showing how often getattr is called: ls → getattr for each file; tab-complete → getattr; vim opening → multiple getattr calls; find command → getattr for every path. Diagram emphasizes this must be O(1) and correct.

diagrams.title: Path Resolution in FUSE Callbacks
diagrams.description: Flow diagram: callback receives '/a/b/c' → parse into components ['a', 'b', 'c'] → resolve_path(root_inode, components) → returns inode number → operate on inode. Cache optimization: dentry cache can skip repeated resolutions.

diagrams.title: Thread Safety: Locking Strategy
diagrams.description: Structure layout showing lock granularity: global filesystem lock (for bitmap operations), per-inode locks (for read/write), directory lock (for add/remove entries). Deadlock prevention: always acquire locks in order: global → directory → inode.

diagrams.title: rename: Atomic Cross-Directory Move
diagrams.description: State evolution for rename('/a/file', '/b/file'): (1) acquire locks on both parent directories, (2) remove entry from /a, (3) add entry to /b, (4) release locks. Must be atomic: crash between steps 2-3 must not lose the file. Journal wraps this in a transaction.

diagrams.title: Mount and Unmount Lifecycle
diagrams.description: Sequence diagram: mount → fuse_main() → init callback (initialize filesystem state) → event loop (receive callbacks). Unmount → destroy callback (flush dirty data, close block device) → exit. SIGTERM triggers clean unmount.

milestones.title: Write-Ahead Journaling and Crash Recovery
milestones.summary: Implement write-ahead journaling for crash consistency. All metadata operations are logged before being applied, enabling recovery from crashes without corruption. Build transaction begin/commit/abort, journal replay on mount, and crash simulation tests.
milestones.misconception: Developers think 'crash safety' means 'data survives a crash'. In filesystem terms, crash safety means 'the filesystem metadata is consistent after a crash'—not that your data is preserved. Metadata journaling protects the filesystem structure (bitmaps, inodes, directories) but not necessarily file contents. A crash mid-write might lose the last few KB of data, but the filesystem will still mount and the rest of your files will be intact.
milestones.reveal: The journal is a circular buffer of transaction records. Each operation (create file, write to inode, allocate block) is logged as 'I'm about to do X'. The commit record says 'all the previous operations in this transaction are complete'. On crash recovery: scan the journal, find committed transactions, replay them (idempotently). Uncommitted transactions are discarded. The key insight: data is written to the journal BEFORE being written to the main filesystem. On crash, either the journal has the operation (replay it) or it doesn't (operation never happened). No partial state.
cascade: Database WAL (PostgreSQL, SQLite, MySQL) — the exact same technique. Database transactions are logged before page modifications, enabling crash recovery and point-in-time recovery.
Message queue durability (Kafka, RabbitMQ) — messages are logged to a write-ahead log before being marked as delivered. Enables replay on crash.
Event sourcing and CQRS — the event log is a journal of state changes. Replay events to reconstruct state. Same idempotency requirements.
Distributed consensus (Raft, Paxos) — the consensus log is a write-ahead journal replicated across nodes. Recovery involves replaying committed entries.
Version control (git) — the reflog is a journal of reference updates. Enables recovery from accidental resets or deletes.
yaml_acceptance_criteria: Journal region occupies a fixed set of blocks on disk (configured in superblock); journal entries are written sequentially with wrap-around
Each filesystem operation (create, write metadata, mkdir, unlink, etc.) is wrapped in a transaction: begin → journal writes → commit → apply to data structures → end
Transaction commit writes a commit record to the journal; without a commit record, the transaction is considered incomplete on recovery
Recovery (fsck/replay): on mount, scan the journal for committed transactions and replay their operations; discard uncommitted transactions
Metadata journaling mode: only metadata changes (inode updates, bitmap changes, directory entries) are journaled; data blocks are written directly
Crash simulation test: kill the filesystem process mid-operation, remount, verify recovery produces a consistent filesystem with no orphaned inodes or leaked blocks
Journal checkpoint: after all committed transactions are applied, the journal is marked as clean so it doesn't replay stale entries

diagrams.title: Journal Region Layout
diagrams.description: Street view showing journal blocks: header block (sequence number, start/end offsets), followed by transaction blocks (T1: begin, operations..., commit; T2: begin, operations..., commit). Circular: when reaching end, wrap to beginning (after checkpoint).

diagrams.title: Transaction: Begin, Journal, Commit, Apply, End
diagrams.description: State evolution: (1) begin: allocate transaction ID, (2) journal_write: log each operation (inode update, bitmap change, dirent add), (3) commit: write commit record, fsync journal, (4) apply: perform actual operations on filesystem, (5) end: mark transaction complete in journal.

diagrams.title: Commit Record: The Atomic Marker
diagrams.description: Microscopic view of commit record: transaction_id (8B), checksum (4B), timestamp (8B), status='COMMITTED' (1B). The checksum covers all operations in the transaction. Partial commit records fail checksum on recovery.

diagrams.title: Crash Recovery: Replay Flow
diagrams.description: Flow diagram: mount → read journal header → scan from start to end → for each transaction: if committed, replay operations; if not committed, skip → after scan, checkpoint journal → filesystem ready.

diagrams.title: Idempotent Operations for Safe Replay
diagrams.description: Before/after showing operation design: BAD: 'increment free_blocks' (not idempotent, replay doubles the increment). GOOD: 'set free_blocks = 1234' (idempotent, replay sets same value). All journaled operations must be state-setting, not state-modifying.

diagrams.title: Crash Scenarios and Outcomes
diagrams.description: Decision matrix: (1) crash before commit → transaction incomplete, discarded on recovery, no changes applied. (2) crash after commit, before apply → recovery replays transaction, changes applied. (3) crash during apply → recovery replays, idempotent ops ensure correct final state.

diagrams.title: Journal Checkpoint Process
diagrams.description: State evolution: journal full of committed transactions → checkpoint: verify all applied → write new journal header with start=0, sequence=next → journal now 'empty' for new transactions. Enables wrap-around without losing data.

diagrams.title: Metadata-Only vs Full Data Journaling
diagrams.description: Comparison diagram: Metadata-only journals inode/bitmap/dirent changes, data blocks written directly. Faster but data loss possible. Full journaling journals data blocks too. Slower (2x writes) but data preserved. Linux ext4 defaults to metadata-only for performance.

diagrams.title: Filesystem Component Atlas
diagrams.description: Satellite view showing all six milestones as interconnected regions: Block Layer (foundation) supports Inode Management (file metadata) and Directory Operations (name resolution), which together enable File Read/Write (data access), all exposed through FUSE Integration (OS interface), with Journaling (crash safety) wrapping all metadata operations.

diagrams.title: Filesystem Layer Stack
diagrams.description: Vertical stack diagram: Top: Applications (ls, cat, vim). Below: FUSE (userspace↔kernel bridge). Below: VFS operations (create, read, write, mkdir). Below: Journaling wrapper (transactions). Below: Inode + Directory layer. Below: Block allocator (bitmaps). Bottom: Block device (disk image). Arrows show call flow.

diagrams.title: Cache Levels and Miss Penalties
diagrams.description: Hardware soul view: L1 cache (4 cycles, 64B lines) → L2 cache (12 cycles) → L3 cache (40 cycles) → RAM (100ns) → SSD (100μs) → HDD (10ms). Filesystem operations target RAM and SSD; block cache keeps hot blocks in memory. Bitmap operations should fit in L2/L3.

tdd_blueprint.design_vision: A complete inode-based filesystem with block allocation, directory tree management, FUSE integration, and write-ahead journaling for crash consistency. The design embodies the fundamental tension between logical file abstractions (paths, byte streams) and physical storage reality (blocks, sectors, disk latency). Each layer negotiates with hardware constraints: 4KB block alignment matches SSD pages and memory pages, bitmap allocation trades scan time for constant-time free/used tracking, indirect pointers balance inode size against maximum file size, and journaling transforms random metadata writes into sequential log entries for both crash safety and write performance.

modules.name: Block Layer and mkfs
modules.description: Implements raw block device abstraction over a file-backed image, defines the complete on-disk layout (superblock, bitmaps, inode table, journal, data blocks), provides bitmap-based allocation for blocks and inodes, and creates the mkfs formatter that breathes structure into empty space. Does NOT implement inode contents, directory operations, or journaling logic.

specs.inputs: Raw file path, size specification (blocks or MB), filesystem parameters (inode count, journal size)
specs.outputs: Formatted filesystem image with valid superblock at block 0, initialized bitmaps, zeroed inode table with root directory inode, empty journal region, and consistent free counts
specs.abstractions: BlockDevice (read_block/write_block over file), Superblock (filesystem metadata at fixed location), Bitmap (bit-level allocation tracking), FileSystem (aggregate of device + superblock + buffers)
error_categories: E_INVALID_IMAGE - File cannot be created or resized
E_BAD_ALIGNMENT - Offset not divisible by block size
specs.concurrency_model: Single-threaded for mkfs; subsequent milestones add locking
performance_targets: Block read: <1μs for cached, <25μs for NVMe, <10ms for HDD
Bitmap allocation: O(n) scan where n = bitmap blocks, typically 1-2 blocks
mkfs 100MB image: <100ms

hardware_soul.access_pattern: Sequential for mkfs, random for allocation
hardware_soul.simd_opportunity: Bitmap scanning could use vectorized population count
hardware_soul.branch_prediction: Predictable in mkfs loops, less predictable in alloc scanning

implementation_phases.name: Block device abstraction
implementation_phases.description: Implement BlockDevice struct with open/read/write/close over file with lseek

implementation_phases.name: Superblock structure
implementation_phases.description: Define Superblock struct with all fields, magic number, write to block 0

implementation_phases.name: Block bitmap allocator
implementation_phases.description: Implement alloc_block/free_block with bitmap scanning using __builtin_ffsll

implementation_phases.name: Inode bitmap allocator
implementation_phases.description: Mirror block bitmap logic for inode numbers

implementation_phases.name: Layout calculation
implementation_phases.description: Calculate bitmap_blocks, inode_table_blocks, journal_blocks from total_blocks

implementation_phases.name: mkfs main function
implementation_phases.description: Orchestrate all initialization, create root directory with . and ..

implementation_phases.name: Verification and testing
implementation_phases.description: Implement verify_filesystem, test round-trip read after write

diagrams.title: Block Device Architecture
diagrams.description: BlockDevice struct with fd, size, num_blocks; read_block/write_block flow showing lseek + read/write syscalls; file-backed image mapping

diagrams.title: On-Disk Layout Map
diagrams.description: Block address space showing: Block 0 (superblock), Blocks 1-N (block bitmap), Blocks N+1-M (inode bitmap), Blocks M+1-K (inode table), Blocks K+1-J (journal), Blocks J+1-end (data blocks)

diagrams.title: Superblock Memory Layout
diagrams.description: Byte offsets and field sizes for all superblock fields: magic@0(4B), version@4(4B), total_blocks@8(8B), ..., padding to 4096B. Cache line boundaries at 64B intervals

diagrams.title: Bitmap Block Structure
diagrams.description: 4KB block containing 32768 bits, each bit representing one block/inode. Word-level view showing uint64_t array[512]. Bit-to-block calculation formula

diagrams.title: Block Allocation Flow
diagrams.description: alloc_block: scan bitmap blocks → find word with ~0 != all_ones → __builtin_ffsll to find bit → set bit → write bitmap block → calculate block number → decrement free_blocks

diagrams.title: mkfs Initialization Sequence
diagrams.description: Step-by-step: create file → write superblock → zero/write block bitmap → zero/write inode bitmap (mark inode 1 used) → zero inode table → create root inode → create root directory block → zero journal

diagrams.title: Root Directory Entry Layout
diagrams.description: First data block containing DirEntry[0]='.' and DirEntry[1]='..', both pointing to inode 1. Byte offsets for each field

diagrams.title: Filesystem Verification State Machine
diagrams.description: States: START → READ_SUPERBLOCK → VALIDATE_MAGIC → COUNT_FREE_BLOCKS → COMPARE_COUNTS → READ_ROOT_INODE → VALIDATE_DIRECTORY → PASS/FAIL. Error transitions on mismatch

diagrams.title: Bitmap Index Calculation
diagrams.description: Visual formula: block_num → data_block_idx = block_num - data_start → bitmap_block_idx = data_block_idx / 32768 → word = (data_block_idx % 32768) / 64 → bit = data_block_idx % 64

diagrams.title: Block Write Data Flow
diagrams.description: write_block(dev, block_num, data): validate bounds → calculate offset = block_num * 4096 → lseek → write 4096 bytes → fsync. Data buffer flows from caller through syscall to file

modules.name: Inode Management
modules.description: Implements the inode structure with metadata fields (mode, uid, gid, size, timestamps, link_count) and block pointer scheme (12 direct + 1 single-indirect + 1 double-indirect). Provides file offset to block number translation, block allocation on write, and complete inode deallocation including recursive freeing of indirect blocks. Does NOT implement directory entry interpretation or file data I/O.

specs.inputs: Inode number, file offsets, data buffers, mode/permission bits
specs.outputs: Inode read/write, block pointer resolution, allocated/freed blocks
specs.abstractions: Inode (fixed-size metadata record), BlockLocation (categorizes offset into direct/indirect zones), IndirectBlock (block containing 512 pointers)
error_categories: E_INODE_INVALID - Inode number out of range
E_NO_SPACE - Block allocation failed during write
E_DOUBLE_FREE - Attempting to free already-free block
specs.concurrency_model: Single-threaded; concurrent access added in M5
performance_targets: Direct block read: 1 block I/O
Single-indirect read: 2 block I/Os
Double-indirect read: 3 block I/Os
Inode serialization: <1μs

hardware_soul.cache_lines_touched: 1 cache line for inode fields, 64 for indirect blocks
hardware_soul.branch_prediction: Zone determination is predictable based on file size
hardware_soul.tlb_misses: Indirect blocks likely cause TLB misses unless cached

implementation_phases.name: Inode structure definition
implementation_phases.description: Define Inode struct with all metadata fields and block pointers, __attribute__((packed))

implementation_phases.name: Zone boundary calculation
implementation_phases.description: Implement locate_block() calculating direct vs single-indirect vs double-indirect zones

implementation_phases.name: Block pointer resolution
implementation_phases.description: Implement get_file_block() traversing direct/indirect/double-indirect pointers

implementation_phases.name: Block allocation on write
implementation_phases.description: Implement get_or_alloc_block() with lazy indirect block allocation

implementation_phases.name: Inode deallocation
implementation_phases.description: Implement inode_free_all_blocks() recursively freeing indirect blocks

implementation_phases.name: Timestamp management
implementation_phases.description: Implement update_timestamps() with atime/mtime/ctime semantics

implementation_phases.name: Testing
implementation_phases.description: Test small files (direct), medium files (single-indirect), large files (double-indirect), sparse files, deallocation

diagrams.title: Inode Structure Memory Layout
diagrams.description: 4096-byte inode showing: mode@0(2B), uid@2(2B), gid@4(2B), link_count@6(2B), size@8(8B), blocks@16(8B), timestamps@24(24B), direct[12]@48(96B), indirect@144(8B), double_ind@152(8B), padding to 4096B

diagrams.title: Block Pointer Zone Atlas
diagrams.description: Three zones: Direct (blocks 0-11, 0-48KB), Single-Indirect (blocks 12-523, 48KB-~2.1MB), Double-Indirect (blocks 524-262667, ~2.1MB-~1GB). Capacity calculations

diagrams.title: Indirect Block Structure
diagrams.description: 4KB block containing uint64_t[512] pointers. Index calculation: ptrs[block_offset_in_zone % 512]

diagrams.title: Block Location Calculation
diagrams.description: locate_block(offset): block_offset = offset / 4096 → if < 12: direct[block_offset] → elif < 12+512: indirect → else: double_ind. Step-by-step arithmetic

diagrams.title: Double-Indirect Traversal
diagrams.description: get_file_block for double-indirect: read double_ind block → double_idx = (offset - direct - single) / (512*512) → read indirect block → indirect_idx = remainder / 512 → return ptrs[indirect_idx]

diagrams.title: Sparse File Hole Detection
diagrams.description: get_file_block returns 0 → caller synthesizes zeros without disk I/O. Visual showing file with holes: allocated blocks at 0 and 1MB, holes in between

diagrams.title: Block Allocation Cascade
diagrams.description: get_or_alloc_block: check existing → if 0: alloc_block → if indirect zone: ensure indirect block exists (alloc if needed) → write pointer to indirect block → return

diagrams.title: Inode Deallocation Tree
diagrams.description: inode_free_all_blocks: for each direct[i]: free_block → if indirect: for each ptr in indirect block: free_block → free indirect block → if double_ind: recursive free

diagrams.title: Link Count State Machine
diagrams.description: States: link_count=0 (unallocated), link_count=1 (single reference), link_count=N (multiple hard links). Transitions: inode_link++ , inode_unlink--. Delete when reaches 0

diagrams.title: File Size vs Block Allocation
diagrams.description: Visual comparison: file size (logical) vs blocks allocated (physical). Sparse file: size=1GB, blocks=3. Dense file: size=1GB, blocks=262144

modules.name: Directory Operations
modules.description: Implements directory entries as name→inode mappings stored in directory file data blocks. Provides path resolution traversing the directory tree component-by-component, directory creation/deletion with link count management, hard link creation, and symbolic link support. Does NOT implement file content I/O or FUSE integration.

specs.inputs: Path strings, directory inode numbers, names, target inodes
specs.outputs: Resolved inode numbers, directory listings, created/removed entries
specs.abstractions: DirEntry (on-disk name→inode record), Path (array of components), DentryCache (future optimization for resolved paths)
error_categories: E_NOT_DIRECTORY - Path component is a regular file
E_EXIST - Name already exists in directory
E_LOOP - Too many symbolic link resolutions
specs.concurrency_model: Single-threaded; concurrent directory modification needs locking
performance_targets: dir_lookup: O(n) scan of directory entries
path_resolve: O(depth × entries_per_directory)
mkdir/rmdir: O(1) bitmap + O(n) directory scan

hardware_soul.simd_opportunity: Name comparison could use vectorized memcmp

implementation_phases.name: Directory entry structure
implementation_phases.description: Define DirEntry struct with inode, rec_len, name_len, file_type, name[256], padding to 280 bytes

diagrams.title: Directory Entry Memory Layout
diagrams.description: 280-byte DirEntry: inode@0(8B), rec_len@8(2B), name_len@10(1B), file_type@11(1B), name@12(256B), padding@268(12B). 14 entries per 4KB block

diagrams.title: Directory as File Concept
diagrams.description: Directory inode pointing to data blocks containing DirEntry array. Visual showing regular file vs directory file interpretation of same inode structure

diagrams.title: Directory Lookup Scan
diagrams.description: dir_lookup: for each block in directory → read block → for each entry → if inode != 0 and name matches → return inode. Linear scan visualization

diagrams.title: Path Resolution Chain
diagrams.description: Path '/a/b/c.txt' resolution: root(1) → lookup 'a' → inode 100 → lookup 'b' → inode 200 → lookup 'c.txt' → inode 300. Each arrow is one dir_lookup

diagrams.title: Special Entries . and ..
diagrams.description: Directory block showing entry[0]='.' pointing to self, entry[1]='..' pointing to parent. Root's .. points to root (cycle termination)

diagrams.title: mkdir Operation Sequence
diagrams.description: fs_mkdir: alloc_inode → init inode with S_IFDIR → dir_add_entry for '.' → dir_add_entry for '..' → dir_add_entry in parent → increment parent link_count

diagrams.title: Link Count State Transitions
diagrams.description: Directory link count state machine: new dir=2 (parent entry + .), add subdir=3 (+1 for child's ..), remove subdir=2. Regular file: create=1, hard link=2, unlink=1 or 0

diagrams.title: rmdir Empty Check
diagrams.description: dir_is_empty: scan directory → count entries where name != '.' and name != '..' → if count > 0: return false

diagrams.title: Hard Link vs Symbolic Link
diagrams.description: Hard link: dir entry A → inode X ← dir entry B. Symlink: dir entry C → symlink inode → path string → resolve to target inode

diagrams.title: Rename Atomic Move
diagrams.description: fs_rename: lookup old path → lookup new parent → add entry in new location → remove entry from old location → update .. if directory move. All must succeed or rollback

diagrams.title: Directory Entry Add Flow
diagrams.description: dir_add_entry: check duplicate → scan for free slot (inode=0) → if found: write entry → if not found: extend directory with new block → write entry → update directory size

diagrams.title: Path Component Split
diagrams.description: split_path('/home/user/file.txt'): skip leading '/' → extract 'home' → extract 'user' → extract 'file.txt' → return ['home', 'user', 'file.txt']

modules.name: File Read/Write Operations
modules.description: Implements file creation, reading, writing, truncation, and sparse file handling. Translates logical byte offsets to physical block numbers, handles block boundary crossing, allocates blocks on demand during writes, synthesizes zeros for holes, and updates metadata (size, timestamps) correctly. Does NOT implement FUSE integration or journaling.

specs.inputs: Path or inode number, byte offsets, data buffers, truncation sizes
specs.outputs: Bytes read/written, file metadata updates, allocated/freed blocks
specs.abstractions: FileHandle (inode + current offset, future), Hole (unallocated region reading as zeros), SparseFile (file with holes)
error_categories: E_ISDIR - Operation not permitted on directory
E_NOSPC - No space left on device
specs.concurrency_model: Single-threaded; concurrent file access needs per-inode locking
performance_targets: Sequential read: ~90% of raw block device speed
Random read: dominated by seek latency
Sparse file hole read: 0 disk I/O, memset only
Truncate extend: metadata update only, no block allocation

hardware_soul.access_pattern: Sequential for large reads/writes, random for sparse access

implementation_phases.description: Integrate get_or_alloc_block from M2, handle indirect block allocation

implementation_phases.name: Truncate grow

implementation_phases.name: Truncate shrink

implementation_phases.name: Sparse file handling

implementation_phases.name: Append operation

implementation_phases.name: Testing

diagrams.title: Read Loop Block Boundary
diagrams.description: Read 8000 bytes at offset 3000: Block 0[3000-4095]=1096B → Block 1[0-4095]=4096B → Block 2[0-2807]=2808B. Three iterations visualized

diagrams.title: Write Read-Modify-Write
diagrams.description: Partial block write at offset 4050: read block 42 → modify bytes 4050-4095 → write block 42. Full block write: direct write, no read

diagrams.title: Sparse File Allocation
diagrams.description: Write at 10MB offset in empty file: allocate double_ind block → allocate indirect block → allocate data block. 3 blocks for 1 write

diagrams.title: Hole Detection Flow
diagrams.description: fs_read: get_file_block returns 0 → memset zeros to buffer → no disk I/O. Visual showing logical file vs physical blocks

diagrams.title: Truncate Shrink Block Free
diagrams.description: Truncate 16KB to 6KB: keep blocks 0-1, free blocks 2-3, zero bytes 6000-8191 in block 1. Block pointer updates

diagrams.title: File Size vs Allocated Blocks
diagrams.description: State diagram: size field (logical boundary) vs blocks count (physical allocation). Truncate grow: size++, blocks unchanged. Truncate shrink: size--, blocks--

diagrams.title: File Write Data Flow
diagrams.description: fs_write(inode, offset, data, len): read inode → for each block: get_or_alloc_block → read existing (if partial) → modify → write block → update inode size/mtime

diagrams.title: Block Allocation During Write
diagrams.description: Write triggering allocation: offset in direct zone → alloc_block → store in direct[i]. Offset in indirect zone → ensure indirect block → alloc_block → store pointer

diagrams.title: Metadata Update Matrix
diagrams.description: Table showing operation vs timestamp updates: read→atime, write→mtime+ctime, truncate→mtime+ctime, chmod→ctime only

diagrams.title: File I/O Three-Level View
diagrams.description: Application layer (fs_read call) → OS/Kernel layer (page cache, syscall) → Hardware layer (disk controller, DMA). Latency at each level

modules.name: FUSE Integration
modules.description: Mounts the filesystem via FUSE as a real mount point accessible to all OS programs. Implements all required FUSE callbacks (getattr, readdir, create, open, read, write, mkdir, rmdir, unlink, rename, truncate, chmod, utimens, statfs) with proper error codes. Handles concurrent access with thread-safe locking. Does NOT implement journaling.

specs.inputs: FUSE callbacks with path strings and fuse_file_info structures
specs.outputs: Stat structures, directory listings, file data, error codes
specs.abstractions: FUSE Daemon (userspace process handling VFS callbacks), FileHandle (fi->fh storing inode number), MountPoint (directory where filesystem is accessible)
error_categories: ENOENT - No such file or directory
ENOSPC - No space left on device
specs.concurrency_model: Multi-threaded by default; global pthread_mutex for all operations (coarse-grained), or per-inode locks (fine-grained)
performance_targets: getattr: <100μs with warm cache
readdir 100 entries: <10ms
Sequential read/write: within 50% of native filesystem
FUSE overhead: 2-6× slower than kernel filesystem

hardware_soul.cache_lines_touched: Path string + stat structure per call
hardware_soul.access_pattern: Path resolution repeated on every call without caching

diagrams.title: FUSE Architecture
diagrams.description: Application → VFS (kernel) → FUSE module → /dev/fuse → FUSE daemon (userspace) → filesystem code. Context switches at kernel boundary

diagrams.title: FUSE Callback Flow
diagrams.description: cat /mnt/file.txt: VFS → FUSE: getattr('/mnt/file.txt') → daemon resolves path → fills stat → returns → FUSE: open() → FUSE: read() → FUSE: release()

diagrams.title: File Handle Usage
diagrams.description: fs_open: fi->fh = inode_num. fs_read: inode_num = fi->fh → skip path resolution. fs_release: clear fi->fh

diagrams.title: readdir Filler Callback
diagrams.description: fs_readdir: for each entry → filler(buf, name, &stat, 0) → if returns 1: buffer full. Iteration until all entries or buffer full

diagrams.title: Error Code Mapping
diagrams.description: Table: filesystem error → FUSE return value. path_resolve returns 0 → -ENOENT. dir_lookup finds existing → -EEXIST. alloc_inode returns 0 → -ENOSPC

diagrams.title: Thread Locking Strategy
diagrams.description: Coarse-grained: single pthread_mutex for all operations. Fine-grained: per-inode locks + bitmap lock + superblock lock. Lock ordering to prevent deadlock

diagrams.title: Mount Lifecycle State Machine
diagrams.description: States: UNMOUNTED → parse_args → OPEN_FS → VALIDATE_SB → fuse_main → MOUNTED → (operations) → unmount signal → fs_destroy → UNMOUNTED

diagrams.title: Concurrent Access Scenario
diagrams.description: Thread A: mkdir /dir1. Thread B: mkdir /dir2. Both check parent, both allocate inode, both add entry. Without lock: race condition. With lock: serialized

diagrams.title: stat Structure Fill
diagrams.description: fs_getattr filling struct stat: st_ino, st_mode, st_nlink, st_uid, st_gid, st_size, st_blocks, st_atime, st_mtime, st_ctime, st_blksize. Field-by-field mapping from inode

diagrams.title: FUSE Performance Overhead
diagrams.description: Breakdown of 100μs getattr: path resolution (20μs), inode read (10μs), stat fill (1μs), context switches (2× 20μs), FUSE protocol (29μs)

modules.name: Write-Ahead Journaling and Crash Recovery
modules.description: Implements a write-ahead journal for crash consistency. All metadata operations (inode allocation, block allocation, directory modifications, superblock updates) are logged to a sequential journal before being applied. On mount after a crash, committed transactions are replayed to restore consistency. Uses metadata-only journaling (data blocks written directly). Does NOT implement full data journaling.

specs.outputs: Committed journal entries, recovered consistent filesystem after crash
specs.abstractions: Journal (circular buffer of transactions), Transaction (begin/log/commit/apply/end lifecycle), JournalEntry (typed operation record), Checkpoint (journal clearing after apply)
error_categories: E_JOURNAL_FULL - No space in journal, need checkpoint
specs.concurrency_model: Single active transaction; commit serializes with fsync
performance_targets: Journal write: sequential, ~90% of raw sequential write speed
Transaction commit: 1 fsync (~1-10ms depending on storage)
Recovery replay: O(committed transactions) × operation apply time
Metadata write amplification: 1× (journal only, not double)

hardware_soul.access_pattern: Sequential writes to journal (optimal for HDD and SSD)

implementation_phases.description: Define JournalHeader, JournalEntry types, Transaction struct

implementation_phases.description: Implement journal_read_header, journal_write_header with checksum
